## Hyperplanes 

## Planes in 3D

In $\mathbb{R}^3$, a plane is a collection of points lying on a flat surface. 
If the plane passes through the origin, then it forms a subspace.

Given two linearly independent vectors $u$ and $v$, we can linearly combine them to generate a plane $\Pi$ 
$$
\Pi = \{ x \mid x = \alpha u + \beta v,\; \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R} \}
$$

It thus holds that $\Pi = \text{span}\{u, v\}$, and the plane is oriented in the directions of $u$ and $v$.

For any plane, there exists a normal vector $N$ such that for any point $p \in \Pi$:

$$N \cdot p = 0$$

This means that $N$ is orthogonal to every vector in $\Pi$, typically written as $N \perp \Pi$.
In $\mathbb{R}^3$, since $p \in \text{span}\{u, v\}$, we can define the normal vector as $N = u \times v$.
This way, by construction, $N$ is orthogonal to both $u$ and $v$.

If we write $N = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{pmatrix}$ and $p = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix}$, then the condition $N \cdot p = 0$ becomes:

$$
ax + by + cz = 0
$$

This is the Cartesian equation of the plane.

We have one equation with three unknowns, so the solution space is two-dimensional.
This is consistent with our initial description saying that $\Pi \in \text{span}\{u, v\}$.
In particular, we can isolate $z$ in the cartesian form and re-write $p$, to prove that $u = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ -\frac{a}{c}\end{pmatrix}$ and $v = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -\frac{b}{c}\end{pmatrix}$.


## Uncentered 3D-planes 

In the most general case, we can explicitly highlight that plane $\Pi$ passes through point $p_0$

$$
\Pi = \{ x \mid x = p_0 + \alpha u + \beta v,\; \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R} \}
$$

If $p_0 \notin \text{span}\{u, v\}$, then we have a plane that does not cross the origin anymore.

To account for the existence of $p_0$ in our previous formulas, we only need to make an affine transformation.
We will now have

$$N \cdot (p - p_0) = 0$$

which highlights that $N$ is orthogonal to any direction contained in $\Pi$.
Moreover, we now have $N \cdot P = N \cdot p_0$, so then the cartesian equation becomes

$$
\Pi: ax + by + cz + d = 0
$$

where $d = - N \cdot p_0$.
As we will explore later, $d$ is proportional to the distance from the plane to the origin.

## Hyperplanes

In $\mathbb{R}^n$, we define an hyperplane following a similar idea:

$$
N \cdot (p - p_0) = 0
$$

We now have $n$ unknowns, and for $N = \begin{pmatrix}a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_n \end{pmatrix}$ it holds that:

$$a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 + \dots + a_n x_n + d = 0$$

Since one linear constraint is imposed, the hyperplane lives in a space of dimension n - 1.
Similar to before, $N$ is orthogonal to the hyperplane.
 
$$\text{dist}(x, \Pi) = \frac{|N \cdot x + d|}{\|N\|}$$
